icebreaker.text: If you could have any superpower for one workday, what would it be?
What's a skill you'd love to learn this year?
What's the best meal you've had recently?
If you could live in any city for a month, where would you go?
What's a book or show you'd recommend to the team?
What's your go-to comfort food?
If you could have lunch with any historical figure, who would it be?
What's the most interesting thing you've learned recently?
What's your favorite way to unwind after work?
If you could instantly become an expert in something, what would you choose?
What's a hobby you picked up as an adult?
What's the best piece of advice you've ever received?
If you could travel back in time to witness one event, what would it be?
What's a small thing that makes your day better?
What's your favorite season and why?
If you could have any animal as a pet (and it was safe and happy), what would you pick?
What's something on your bucket list?
What's the most adventurous thing you've ever done?
If you could master any musical instrument overnight, which one would it be?
What's your favorite thing about where you live?
What was your dream job as a kid?
What's a movie you could watch over and over?
What's a food you thought you wouldn't like but ended up loving?
What's your favorite way to start the morning?
If you could design your perfect home office, what would it include?
What's the best trip you've ever taken?
What's a fun tradition you have with family or friends?
If you could switch lives with anyone for a day, who would you pick and why?
What's something you're looking forward to this month?
What is one tiny upgrade that would make your workday better?
What is a hill you are willing to die on about snacks, coffee, or lunch?
What is a tool, shortcut, or habit that feels like a cheat code?
What is something you are weirdly good at?
What is your favorite low-effort way to reset your brain?
If your week had a theme song, what would it be?
What is something small that made you laugh recently?
What is a movie, game, book, or show you wish you could experience again for the first time?
What is a skill you learned outside of work that unexpectedly helped you at work?
What is your default beverage for getting things done?
If you could automate one boring life task, what would it be?
What is a place you have been that felt underrated?
What is your favorite thing to do when you unexpectedly get an extra hour?
What is something you loved as a kid that still holds up?
What is your most used app that is not Slack, email, or a browser?
If your desktop or workspace could talk, what would it say about you?
What is a food combination you defend completely?
What is something you have changed your mind about recently?
What is one thing you wish more people knew about your role?
What is a tiny luxury that improves your day every time?
If you had to give a five-minute talk on anything, what topic would you pick?
What is a random fact you know that lives rent-free in your brain?
What is your favorite way to celebrate finishing something difficult?
What is one thing you are looking forward to outside of work?
What is your personal sign that you need a break?
What is a recommendation you gave recently that you stand by?
If you could instantly make one meeting habit universal, what would it be?
What is something you are currently trying to get better at?
What is one thing that made this week easier than it could have been?

funfact.text: Honey never spoils. Archaeologists have found 3,000-year-old honey in Egyptian tombs that's still edible.
Octopuses have three hearts and blue blood.
A group of flamingos is called a 'flamboyance.'
Bananas are berries, but strawberries aren't.
The shortest war in history lasted 38 minutes (Britain vs. Zanzibar, 1896).
There are more possible iterations of a game of chess than there are atoms in the observable universe.
Cows have best friends and get stressed when separated.
The inventor of the Pringles can is buried in one.
A day on Venus is longer than a year on Venus.
Wombat poop is cube-shaped.
The Hawaiian pizza was invented in Canada.
Sea otters hold hands while sleeping so they don't drift apart.
The average person walks about 100,000 miles in a lifetime — roughly four trips around the Earth.
There's a species of jellyfish that is biologically immortal.
Scotland's national animal is the unicorn.
A cloud can weigh more than a million pounds.
The first oranges weren't orange — they were green.
Sloths can hold their breath longer than dolphins can.
There are more trees on Earth than stars in the Milky Way.
The dot over the letters 'i' and 'j' is called a tittle.
Butterflies taste with their feet.
An astronaut's footprints on the Moon could last millions of years.
The longest hiccuping spree lasted 68 years.
Polar bears have black skin under their white fur.
A bolt of lightning is five times hotter than the surface of the sun.
Mercury is the fastest planet, completing an orbit around the Sun in just 88 Earth days.
A day on Venus is longer than a year on Venus.
Mars has nearly the same surface area as Earth's dry land, even though Mars is much smaller overall.
Olympus Mons on Mars is the largest volcano in the solar system.
Jupiter has the shortest day of any planet in the solar system, spinning once in about 9.9 hours.
If Jupiter were hollow, about 1,000 Earths could fit inside it.
Jupiter's Great Red Spot is a storm that has been observed for more than 300 years.
Saturn's rings stretch up to about 175,000 miles from the planet, but the main rings are typically only about 30 feet thick.
Saturn is the only planet in our solar system with an average density lower than water.
Uranus rotates almost sideways, making it look like it rolls around the Sun.
A year on Uranus lasts about 84 Earth years.
Uranus has moons named after characters from Shakespeare and Alexander Pope.
Neptune is more than 30 times farther from the Sun than Earth is.
Neptune completed its first orbit since being discovered in 1846 in the year 2011.
The Moon is slowly moving away from Earth by about 1.5 inches each year.
Humans did not see the far side of the Moon until a spacecraft flew past it in 1959.
About 30 Earth-sized planets could fit between Earth and the Moon.
Europa, one of Jupiter's moons, likely has a salty ocean beneath its icy shell.
Europa may hold about twice as much water as Earth's global ocean.
Titan is the only moon in our solar system known to have a substantial atmosphere.
Titan has rivers, lakes, and seas, but they are made of liquid methane and ethane instead of water.
Enceladus, a moon of Saturn, sprays water vapor and ice particles into space.
Ceres is the only dwarf planet located in the inner solar system.
Ceres was the first dwarf planet visited by a spacecraft.
Haumea spins so quickly that its shape is stretched out like a football.
The ocean covers about 70 percent of Earth's surface.
About 97 percent of Earth's water is in the ocean.
Hubble travels around Earth at about 17,000 miles per hour and completes an orbit roughly every 95 minutes.
Voyager 1 is the first human-made object to enter interstellar space.
Asteroid Bennu is only about as wide as the Empire State Building is tall.
In ideal conditions, giant kelp can grow up to 18 inches in a single day.
Kelp forests provide food and shelter for thousands of marine species.
Seamounts are underwater mountains that can act like oases of life in the deep ocean.
Hydrothermal vents form when seawater seeps into cracks in the ocean crust and gets heated deep below the seafloor.
Coral reefs cover less than 1 percent of the ocean floor but support about 25 percent of marine creatures.
Some deep-sea corals live more than 10,000 feet below sea level, where sunlight barely reaches or does not reach at all.
Estuaries are where river water mixes with seawater, creating nursery habitat for many young fish and shellfish.
Earth's lithosphere is broken into about 15 to 20 moving tectonic plates.
Earth's land masses move toward and away from each other at an average rate of about 1.5 centimeters per year.
The oldest rock ever found is more than 3.9 billion years old.

joke.text: Why do programmers prefer dark mode? Because light attracts bugs.
I told my computer I needed a break, and it said 'No problem — I'll go to sleep.'
Why don't scientists trust atoms? Because they make up everything.
What do you call a fake noodle? An impasta.
Why did the scarecrow win an award? Because he was outstanding in his field.
I'm reading a book about anti-gravity. It's impossible to put down.
What do you call a bear with no teeth? A gummy bear.
Why do cows wear bells? Because their horns don't work.
What did the ocean say to the beach? Nothing, it just waved.
I used to hate facial hair, but then it grew on me.
Why don't eggs tell jokes? They'd crack each other up.
What do you call a lazy kangaroo? A pouch potato.
I'm on a seafood diet. I see food and I eat it.
What did one wall say to the other? I'll meet you at the corner.
Why do bicycles fall over? Because they're two-tired.
What's a tree's favorite drink? Root beer.
I would tell you a construction joke, but I'm still working on it.
What do you call a fish without eyes? A fsh.
Why did the coffee file a police report? It got mugged.
What do dentists call their x-rays? Tooth pics.
I tried to start a hot air balloon business, but it never took off.
What did the grape do when it got stepped on? Nothing, it just let out a little wine.
Why do seagulls fly over the sea? Because if they flew over the bay, they'd be bagels.
I told my wife she was drawing her eyebrows too high. She looked surprised.
What's the best thing about Switzerland? I don't know, but the flag is a big plus.

A QA tester walks into a bar and orders one beer. Then zero beers. Then 999999 beers. Then -1 beers. Then a null beer. The first real customer walks in and asks where the bathroom is, and the bar explodes.
Why did the Java developer need glasses? She couldn't C#.
Why did the QA tester bring a ladder to standup? They heard the build had high-level issues.
A developer says, 'It works on my machine.' QA says, 'Great, ship your machine.'
Why did the bug refuse to reproduce? It wanted better test coverage first.
Why did the programmer quit their job? They didn't get arrays.
QA found a bug in production. The developer said, 'Impossible.' QA replied, 'That's what makes it production.'
Why was the database query so calm? It had already joined all its tables.
Why did the frontend developer break up with the backend developer? Too many unresolved promises.
How does QA make tea? They test with hot water, cold water, no water, too much water, and then ask why the mug accepts strings.
Why did the commit go to therapy? It had too many unresolved conflicts.
Why did the API feel misunderstood? Everyone kept taking its responses out of context.
Why did the developer love dark mode? It made the bugs feel less seen.
Why did the test fail only on CI? Because it wanted a larger audience.
What did the linter say to the messy function? 'We need to talk about your spacing.'
Why did the null pointer apply for a job? It wanted to make a meaningful reference.
Why was the sprint board so dramatic? Everything was either blocked, in progress, or emotionally in review.
Why did the regex get invited to fewer meetings? Nobody wanted to ask what it meant.
QA asked if the field accepted emojis. The developer said, 'Why would anyone do that?' QA smiled and opened Slack.
Why did the programmer count from zero? Because starting at one felt like scope creep.
Why did the deployment bring snacks? It knew it might be a rollback party.
A developer wrote 'temporary fix' in a comment. The codebase quietly celebrated its new permanent resident.
Why did the unit test avoid the integration test? It wasn't ready for a serious relationship.
Why did the ticket come back from QA? It missed everyone and wanted to spend more time with the team.
Why did the CSS rule feel ignored? It had important declarations.
Why did the server log blush? Someone finally read it.
QA said the feature was ready. The developer asked, 'Ready ready, or ticket ready?' Everyone stared into the distance.
